perturbation.original_text: (xxxi) "NON GENERIC EQUIPMENT" - functional testing equipment and any equipment related thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: (xxxi) "NON GENERIC EQUIPMENT" - Equipment used in the manufacturing process.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the definition of "NON GENERIC EQUIPMENT" to a broader definition that only states 'Equipment used in the manufacturing process.' removes the specification that it is related to 'functional testing equipment'. This creates ambiguity and potential contradictions because Section 11.5 details the management and ownership of the Non Generic Equipment and how it should be used, but now it is not very clear what exactly it is. Also in section 1.2.2 Contractor shall assemble its own workstations using its generic equipment and the Non Generic Equipment to be provided by NICE in good working order. The maintenance of the Non Generic Equipment and keeping it in good working order, except normal wear and tear, shall be Contractor's responsibility, at Contractor's expense.
perturbation.contradicted_text: During the term of this Agreement, the Non Generic Equipment shall be furnished to Contractor (without charge) and used in Contractor's premises for purposes of performing its various obligations under this Agreement, according to the terms hereof. Such Non Generic Equipment shall: (1) Be clearly marked and identified as NICE' property. (2) Be safely stored, adequately maintained and insured against loss or damage under Contractor's existing policies detailed in APPENDIX D. In the event such Non Generic Equipment is damaged, lost or destroyed, Contractor shall be liable towards NICE to repair or replace such equipment (at Contractor's choice). (3) Remain the sole property of NICE, and therefore shall be kept free of liens and encumbrances imposed on Contractor's property. (4) Be returned to NICE upon request, or upon termination of this Agreement, at the same condition as originally furnished to Contractor except for normal wear and tear. Notwithstanding anything to contrary, if NICE requests to have the Non Generic Equipment returned thereto prior to the termination of the relevant services hereunder, Contractor shall have no liability or obligation for the performance of any obligations hereunder for which such Non Generic Equipment is reasonably required. Contractor shall notify NICE in writing in detail of the aforementioned, promptly upon NICE' request to receive the Non Generic Equipment. (5) Be used according to NICE' written instructions and information concerning such Non Generic Equipment. (6) Shall not be used by Contractor for any other purpose except for NICE' needs and shall be dedicated for performance of this Agreement. (7) Nevertheless, NICE may request Contractor to purchase some Non Generic Equipment by itself and in such event Contractor shall invoice NICE for the purchase price. Such equipment shall be deemed for all purposes as part of the "Non Generic Equipment" and shall be sold to NICE upon termination of this Agreement for any reason, at a total sale price of 1$.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'NON GENERIC EQUIPMENT', removing the specific requirement that it relates to 'functional testing equipment'. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, which details the use and management of 'Non Generic Equipment' provided by NICE for specific obligations. The broader definition makes it unclear which equipment is subject to the obligations outlined in the contradicted text, potentially leading to disputes about NICE's ownership, the contractor's maintenance responsibilities, and the equipment's permitted uses. Specifically, the original definition tied the equipment to 'functional testing', which now is absent. This contradicts the obligations related to 'NON GENERIC EQUIPMENT', as the parties will not know what obligations exactly relate to that equipment, thus impacting its use as described in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: 12.3. In addition, Contractor shall not disclose the NICE Proprietary Information to any Personnel, except on a need to know basis as required in order to implement this Agreement. Contractor undertakes to procure that its Personnel and Permitted Subcontractors engaged in performance of this Agreement (except subcontractor's employees who do not have access to the NICE production lines at Contractor's premises), and reasonably designated by NICE in advance in writing, have signed a non-disclosure agreement in the form of APPENDIX E, prior to receipt of any NICE Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.3. In addition, Contractor shall not disclose the NICE Proprietary Information to any Personnel, except on a need to know basis as required in order to implement this Agreement. Contractor is encouraged to procure that its Personnel and Permitted Subcontractors engaged in performance of this Agreement have signed a non-disclosure agreement prior to receipt of any NICE Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the binding obligation to ensure that personnel and subcontractors sign a non-disclosure agreement with 'Contractor is encouraged to procure that its Personnel and Permitted Subcontractors engaged in performance of this Agreement have signed a non-disclosure agreement' it weakens the confidentiality protection and contradicts the spirit and legal standing of APPENDIX E, which is a Non Disclosure Undertaking. This also creates a contradiction with section 10.6 Such access shall only be provided to audit personnel who have signed towards Contractor a non-disclosure undertaking incorporating terms which are substantially the same as those in APPENDIX E.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CONTRACTOR RECORD RETENTION AND ACCESS. As part of the Manufacturing Outsourcing Services, Contractor shall (1) retain records and supporting documentation detailed in Section 10.2 above if and to the extent such record retention is required by tax or similar authorities, and/or exists in the ERP system, and/or is common practice in the industry, including but not limited to - production files for the following periods: 7 years for records required by tax or similar authorities and ERP data, 3 years for production files, otherwise as required by law or as is the common practice, and (2) upon notice of no less than five (5) Days from NICE, provide NICE and its designees with reasonable access to such records and documentation for the purpose of conducting NICE' business and reporting. Such access shall only be provided to audit personnel who have signed towards Contractor a non-disclosure undertaking incorporating terms which are substantially the same as those in APPENDIX E.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that the Contractor 'undertakes to procure' signed non-disclosure agreements, whereas the changed text only encourages the Contractor to do so. This weakens the obligation and creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, which states that access shall only be provided to personnel who 'have signed towards Contractor a non-disclosure undertaking incorporating terms which are substantially the same as those in APPENDIX E.' The change from a strict requirement to mere encouragement directly undermines the certainty of the NDA requirement in the contradicted section, creating a strong contradiction. This aligns with the 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' perturbation type because essential language is altered, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 3.4. PERMITTED COMPONENTS. Contractor may make purchase commitments to suppliers and assemble components to sub-assemblies based upon the Forecasts received from NICE but subject always to the Total Lead Time, Liability and ABC policies of Contractor (which NICE will be entitled to review and comment on in advance of the relevant purchase). Contractor shall maintain inventory of Permitted Components (as defined below) for use during the Production Lead Time. NICE shall only be obligated to Contractor for components and sub-assemblies ordered and assembled by Contractor, in accordance with the Forecast and in compliance always with the Total Lead Time as detailed in APPENDIX C (as amended from time to time according to this Agreement) or otherwise for inventory of components purchased from NICE under Sections 2.7 or 3.4(A), or purchased in accordance with POs, ECO's or Change Orders ("PERMITTED Components"), as detailed hereunder. It is clarified that any Forecast updated following the purchase of Permitted Components will not affect their definition as Permitted Components, and NICE' obligations with respect thereto, subject to the Liability.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.4. PERMITTED COMPONENTS. Contractor may make purchase commitments to suppliers and assemble components to sub-assemblies based upon the Forecasts received from NICE. Contractor shall maintain inventory of Permitted Components (as defined below) for use during the Production Lead Time. NICE shall only be obligated to Contractor for components and sub-assemblies ordered and assembled by Contractor, in accordance with the Forecast or otherwise for inventory of components purchased from NICE under Sections 2.7 or 3.4(A), or purchased in accordance with POs, ECO's or Change Orders ("PERMITTED Components"), as detailed hereunder. It is clarified that any Forecast updated following the purchase of Permitted Components will not affect their definition as Permitted Components, and NICE’ obligations with respect thereto.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'but subject always to the Total Lead Time, Liability and ABC policies of Contractor (which NICE will be entitled to review and comment on in advance of the relevant purchase).' from section 3.4 it removes the limitations and NICE's ability to review the ABC policies of Contractor, effectively this perturbation removes key constraints on the Contractor's purchasing behavior. It introduces uncertainty and potential contradictions because later in this section 3.4(A) details how NICE will handle purchases by the Contractor that might be Dead Inventory, Excess Inventory and Slow Moving Inventory and with the removed text it is now harder for NICE to oversee if those purchases are actually being made according to contractually sound reasons. By removing subject always to the Total Lead Time as well, this makes it so the Contractor has no limitations and can buy at whatever time they want which could lead to NICE purchasing something that might not even be needed. It also contradicts section A(xv) "PURCHASE LEAD TIME" - The maximum agreed time for purchase of components by Contractor in order to enable production and completion of a Product until the Due Date, being the total of the time required for ordering and delivering all relevant components to Contractor from Contractor's suppliers, subject to the Liability.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (xv) "PURCHASE LEAD TIME" - The maximum agreed time for purchase of components by Contractor in order to enable production and completion of a Product until the Due Date, being the total of the time required for ordering and delivering all relevant components to Contractor from Contractor's suppliers, subject to the Liability. The initial Purchase Lead Time for each component (including sub-assembly purchased from suppliers) will be as detailed in APPENDIX C and shall be reviewed and updated as necessary by the parties each Quarter during the duration of this Agreement according to the procedure detailed in this Agreement. The new Purchase Lead Time shall need to be agreed to by both parties, and, once agreed, shall be the binding Purchase Lead Time for the relevant components. The parties will also agree on the Purchase Lead Time regarding each new component to be included in a Product.
perturbation.justification: The removal of 'but subject always to the Total Lead Time, Liability and ABC policies of Contractor (which NICE will be entitled to review and comment on in advance of the relevant purchase).' from Section 3.4 directly contradicts the definition of "PURCHASE LEAD TIME" in section A(xv). The original text imposes limitations on the Contractor's purchasing behavior and NICE's ability to review their policies. By removing these constraints, the changed text allows the Contractor to potentially purchase components without regard to the agreed-upon Purchase Lead Time, directly conflicting with the contradicted text which defines Purchase Lead Time as the 'maximum agreed time for purchase of components by Contractor' and details a review and update process. The removal of the 'Total Lead Time' stipulation is key to triggering the contradiction.
